data.title: Is Lumentum’s Recent 90% Rally Warranted After Raised Guidance for 2025?
data.description:
If you have Lumentum Holdings on your radar, you are definitely not alone. It is one of those stocks that seems to keep investors talking, whether they are asking if it is still worth holding after a spectacular run or wondering if now is the right moment to jump in. Over the past year, the stock has soared by 134.5%, with an eye-catching 90.7% gain just since the start of this year. Even the three- and five-year returns have outperformed many peers, climbing 132.1% and 91.4%, respectively.

That being said, this ride has not always been smooth. While last week saw a solid 2.2% uptick, the stock is down by 4.5% over the past month, a reminder that market sentiment and sector trends continue to sway short-term results. Some of this optimism has been linked to broader market strength in technology and communications, but rising interest rates and shifting competitive dynamics have also played a role in shaping investor expectations.

Of course, the big question is whether the current price, now hovering at $163.23 at last close, reflects Lumentum’s true worth. Valuation is where things get interesting. Based on a straightforward scorecard measuring six different checks of undervaluation, Lumentum comes in with a value score of just 0. According to these standard methods, the company does not appear undervalued at all. But before you decide what that means for your portfolio, it is helpful to take a look at what each of these valuation methods actually says and then, at the end, explore a more nuanced approach to gauging true value.

Lumentum Holdings scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Lumentum Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is one of the most widely used methods for estimating a company's intrinsic value. It works by forecasting the company's expected future cash flows and then discounting them back to today's value using a required rate of return. In simple terms, it attempts to answer what those cash flows are worth in today's dollars.

For Lumentum Holdings, the current free cash flow stands at -$43.37 million, which means the company has recently spent more cash than it generated. However, projections indicate a more optimistic outlook. Analyst estimates cover the next five years and, for Lumentum, future free cash flow is expected to steadily grow, reaching around $534.33 million by 2028. Extending the timeline further using conservative growth assumptions, the DCF model incorporates these longer-term projections to estimate the company’s total value.

According to this model, Lumentum’s intrinsic fair value is $143.37 per share. Compared to the current trading price of $163.23, the DCF suggests the stock is about 13.9% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Lumentum Holdings.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Lumentum Holdings may be overvalued by 13.9%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: Lumentum Holdings Price vs Sales

The price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is a preferred valuation method for companies like Lumentum Holdings in the growth stage or those with volatile earnings, as it focuses on overall revenue instead of profits, which may fluctuate year to year. For profitable and established businesses, multiples like the P/S help investors assess whether the stock price makes sense relative to the company's top-line performance.

Normally, growth prospects and industry risk play a big role in determining what a “fair” P/S multiple should be. Companies expected to post higher revenue growth or possessing lower risk profiles often justify higher multiples, while slower-growing or riskier companies tend to receive lower ones.

Lumentum Holdings currently trades at a P/S ratio of 7.04x. For context, that is significantly above the industry average of 2.22x and the average among its peers, which sits at 4.55x. At first glance, this might suggest some level of overvaluation compared to the broader sector.

This is where Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” comes in. Unlike simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio incorporates a range of factors such as Lumentum’s expected growth, risk, profit margin, industry trends, and company size. In this case, the Fair Ratio for Lumentum is calculated at 4.08x, giving a much more customized benchmark for what is “fair” for the stock given its unique characteristics.

Comparing the current P/S of 7.04x to the Fair Ratio of 4.08x, Lumentum trades well above what would be considered reasonable after adjusting for its fundamentals and outlook. This suggests the market may be pricing in more optimism than is warranted by the company’s underlying story.

Result: OVERVALUED

PS ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Lumentum Holdings Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is a clear, evidence-backed story about a company’s future, created by connecting your own view of how the business will perform with specific assumptions about its future revenue, earnings, and margins, ultimately calculating a fair value. Narratives bridge the gap between “just the numbers” and real-world context, letting investors see not just what a company is worth, but why.

You do not need to be a finance pro to use Narratives. Millions of investors on Simply Wall St access and create these on every company’s Community page, helping turn their research into decisions. Narratives allow you to track how your story matches the current share price, showing clearly when the market price is below or above your estimated fair value, so you know if it is time to buy, hold, or sell. In addition, these Narratives automatically update as new news and results arrive, so your perspective grows alongside the business. For example, on Lumentum Holdings, the most bullish Narrative (anchored to booming AI-related demand) estimates a fair value of around $165 per share, while the most cautious Narrative (focused on risk from key customers and supply constraints) sees fair value closer to $83. This demonstrates how different outlooks lead to different actions.

Do you think there's more to the story for Lumentum Holdings? Create your own Narrative to let the Community know!

Wells Fargo’s new coverage boosts market confidence in SPX’s positioning for near-term data center and HVAC momentum, but does not materially alter the biggest short-term catalyst, demand acceleration in data center cooling, or address the current key risk: the lumpiness and potential deceleration of Detection & Measurement segment revenues after major project completions.

Of recent company announcements, the launch of the OlympusV Max data center cooling solution stands out as especially relevant. This product launch supports SPX’s growth catalyst in energy-efficient data centers, potentially accelerating top-line results if it succeeds in differentiating against increased competition and capturing momentum from hyperscale operators.

data.title: Assessing Christian Dior Shares After 16% Rally and Fresh Optimism in Global Luxury Markets
data.description:
If you have been watching Christian Dior's share price recently, you probably noticed some interesting moves. In just the past 30 days, the stock climbed 16.4%, a strong rally that might turn heads even if you have been following the broader luxury sector. Over the past week alone, Dior notched a 6.0% gain, showing renewed confidence or perhaps a change in market sentiment around the brand. That said, when we zoom out, the year-to-date return is still down 6.7%, and the one-year return sits just below zero at -0.6%. Investors who played the long game over the past five years, though, have still seen a rewarding 55.3% total return.

These price movements have been shaped by market-wide optimism toward high-end consumer brands, with Dior benefiting as luxury goods see renewed interest, likely helped by favorable trends in global demand and evolving consumer preferences. While not every blip on the chart has a news headline behind it, it's clear the perception of Dior's risk and growth potential has shifted somewhat recently.

So, does Christian Dior’s recent momentum mean the stock is undervalued, or has the market already priced in all the good news? When we look at valuation, the company scores a 3 out of 6 on our value checklist, indicating potential opportunities but also some caution. In the next section, we will break down these valuation approaches for Dior one by one, and at the end, introduce a more holistic way to think about what the company is really worth.

Why Christian Dior is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: Christian Dior Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates the intrinsic value of a company by forecasting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. This approach helps investors understand what the business is worth based on its ability to generate cash in the future.

For Christian Dior, the DCF analysis uses a two-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The company’s latest reported Free Cash Flow is an impressive €13.5 Billion, with analyst projections indicating steady growth over the coming decade. Over the next ten years, free cash flow is forecast to rise gradually, reaching around €17.7 Billion by 2035. While reliable analyst estimates typically extend only five years, longer-term figures are extrapolated to provide a longer runway for valuation.

Based on these future cash flows, the model calculates a fair value of €1,159 per share. According to this estimate, Dior’s stock is trading at a 51.6% discount. This suggests that the share price is significantly below what the company’s long-term cash generation supports.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Christian Dior.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Christian Dior is undervalued by 51.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Christian Dior Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a strong indicator for profitable companies like Christian Dior, as it shows how investors value the stock relative to its earnings power. For established firms with consistent profits, the PE ratio helps investors weigh current pricing against potential future earnings. This makes it a practical way to assess value beyond just short-term market moves.

A company’s PE ratio reflects not only its current profitability but also market expectations for its future growth and the level of risk investors are willing to bear. Firms with higher growth prospects or lower perceived risk tend to command higher PE ratios, while those facing uncertainty or slower growth generally trade at lower multiples.

Currently, Christian Dior trades on a PE of 22.2x. This is above the luxury sector average of 18.3x, but below the peer group average of 39.2x. While comparing to industry and peer averages is helpful, Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio metric considers not just sector trends, but also Dior’s specific earnings growth, risk profile, profit margins, and market cap to estimate what a truly appropriate PE multiple should be for this company in today’s market. This approach offers a fuller valuation picture than using broad averages alone.

In this case, Dior’s actual PE multiple is very close to its Fair Ratio. This suggests that the stock price is roughly in line with its fundamentals and expected performance.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

PE ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Christian Dior Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let's introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is simply your story about a company, tying together your perspective on its prospects with your estimates for future revenue, earnings, and margins. Narratives empower investors by linking a company’s unique story to a concrete financial forecast, and then translating that outlook into a fair value for the shares.

Available on Simply Wall St’s platform, Narratives are an accessible and practical tool used by millions of investors within the Community page. By comparing your Narrative’s fair value to the current price, you can quickly decide if Christian Dior is a buy, sell, or hold. Your view can adjust as new information or updates emerge. Narratives are regularly updated when relevant news, reports, or earnings releases come in, ensuring your investment thesis remains current.

For example, some investors may be optimistic about Dior’s global expansion, setting a high Narrative value. Others with a more cautious outlook may see risk in changing consumer trends, resulting in a lower value. Narratives make it easy to see and compare these perspectives in real time, giving you a smarter and more dynamic way to invest.

Do you think there's more to the story for Christian Dior? Create your own Narrative to let the Community know!

data.title: Kinnevik (OM:KINV B) Profit Jump Driven by SEK392M One-Off, Challenging Earnings Quality Narratives
data.description:
Kinnevik (OM:KINV B) swung back into profitability over the past year, a turnaround after seeing earnings decline by 59.5% per year during the previous five years. Current forecasts are eye-catching, with revenue set to jump 76.5% per year and EPS forecast for 78.4% annual growth, both outpacing Swedish market averages. However, it is worth noting that a one-off gain of SEK392.0 million in the latest numbers was a factor.

See our full analysis for Kinnevik.

Now, let’s see how these headline figures match up with what the market and community narratives have been expecting, and where the story might unfold differently.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

One-Off SEK392 Million Gain Distorts Profit Picture

  • Kinnevik's latest profit figure was substantially lifted by a single one-off gain of SEK392 million, making this year’s turnaround difficult to compare directly with prior years of declining earnings.
  • Much of the optimism around new profitability comes with a caveat, as the prevailing market view points to:
    • Short-term headline profits boosted by the non-recurring gain, which leaves ongoing profit levels open to question.
    • Despite forecasts of strong future growth, the quality and repeatability of recent profits—not just their size—are key issues for investors, as flagged in financial filings and risk data.

Price-To-Book Ratio Signals Discount vs. Peers

  • Kinnevik trades at a 0.7x Price-To-Book, a clear discount to both its peer average and the European Diversified Financials sector at 1x. This suggests upside potential on a relative valuation basis.
  • The prevailing market view highlights two contrasting threads for value-focused investors:
    • Valuation multiples appear attractive compared to industry and peer norms. This may draw attention from bargain-seeking investors.
    • The discount is likely influenced by caution around earnings quality and the degree to which future high growth forecasts materialize, underscoring why many closely monitor financial momentum rather than headline ratios alone.

Transition to Profitability Puts Focus on Growth Forecasts

  • After years of annual earnings declines averaging 59.5%, Kinnevik achieved profitability in the past year. This shift places investor focus on the sustainability of the projected 76.5% annual revenue growth.
  • The prevailing market view pushes investors to look deeper than the turnaround headline:
    • While the return to profit supports growth narratives, questions remain on whether this momentum can persist without further one-off boosts.
    • With revenue growth projected well above Swedish market averages, execution on portfolio strategy and disciplined capital allocation are critical factors for justifying these forecasts.

See our latest analysis for Kinnevik.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on Kinnevik's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.

See What Else Is Out There

Kinnevik’s turnaround relies heavily on a one-off gain, raising questions around the quality and repeatability of its reported profits and whether growth can be sustained.

data.title: Will Sustained Same-Store Sales Growth Reinforce Ohsho Food Service's (TSE:9936) Competitive Edge?
data.description:
• Ohsho Food Service Corp. reported sales results for September 2025, with all company-operated stores achieving sales of ¥8,243 million (Year-over-year: 103.0%) and existing company-operated stores reaching ¥8,029 million (Year-over-year: 102.4%).
  • This consistent sales momentum, combined with recent financial results showing effective cost management and strong same-store sales, highlights the company's operating resilience in Japan's restaurant sector.
  • We'll explore how sustained same-store sales growth strengthens Ohsho Food Service's investment narrative as it sustains momentum in a competitive market.

What Is Ohsho Food Service's Investment Narrative?

For shareholders, the case for Ohsho Food Service usually comes down to faith in its ability to deliver defensible growth and defend margins in Japan’s increasingly competitive restaurant sector. The company’s latest monthly sales update supports this confidence, with modest same-store sales growth reflecting ongoing traction in a mature market. Importantly, these results come on the heels of a strong earnings report that pushed the stock price up sharply, with evidence of efficient cost controls and steady profit margins. This most recent news may temper concerns around near-term momentum, making the short-term catalysts more about capturing additional market share and less about fighting off a slowdown. However, dividend reductions earlier in the year remain fresh in investors’ minds, and the risk of further cuts, or cost inflation eating away at margins, could resurface if operating conditions change. In the current context, the sales gains provide useful reassurance but don’t fully shift the conversation away from these underlying concerns.
But with dividends recently reduced, there’s still a risk investors should keep top-of-mind.

Ohsho Food Service's shares are on the way up, but they could be overextended by 17%. Uncover the fair value now.

Liquidia's current price-to-book ratio of 131.4x stands in stark contrast to its last close of $23.18, highlighting aggressive market optimism compared to sector standards.

The price-to-book ratio compares a company's market value to its book value and offers insight into how much investors are paying for net assets. In biotech and pharmaceutical sectors, high ratios may reflect faith in future growth, breakthrough products, or pipeline potential. However, they can also signal overheated expectations.

Liquidia's price-to-book ratio is much higher than the peer average of 3.9x and also exceeds the US Pharmaceuticals industry average of 2.4x. This suggests the market is anticipating significant breakthroughs or transformative growth that justifies the steep premium, but it also means the shares are expensive on typical asset-based measures.

data.title: Does First Busey's (BUSE) Dividend Move Reflect Confidence or Conservative Capital Allocation?
data.description:

What Is First Busey's Investment Narrative?

The recent dividend confirmation by First Busey helps anchor its investment case in a period marked by heightened sector optimism and strong results from major peers. Investors here are betting on continued recovery in core profitability and the bank’s ability to benefit from potential monetary policy easing, which often supports regional bank stocks. Yet, the sharp move higher in sector shares this month needs to be set against First Busey’s ongoing challenges, particularly weak net interest margins averaging 3% and analyst forecasts for a 2.1% decline in tangible book value per share over the coming year. While the dividend declaration might provide reassurance about management’s confidence, it does not fundamentally resolve questions about long-term loan profitability or book value pressures. The news likely doesn’t alter most key near-term risks, though a more supportive policy backdrop could shape upside catalysts in coming quarters.

data.title: Bank of Queensland's (ASX:BOQ) Dividend Will Be Increased To A$0.20
data.description:
The board of Bank of Queensland Limited (ASX:BOQ) has announced that it will be paying its dividend of A$0.20 on the 21st of November, an increased payment from last year's comparable dividend. The payment will take the dividend yield to 5.6%, which is in line with the average for the industry.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Bank of Queensland's Payment Expected To Have Solid Earnings Coverage

We like to see a healthy dividend yield, but that is only helpful to us if the payment can continue.

Bank of Queensland has established itself as a dividend paying company with over 10 years history of distributing earnings to shareholders. But while this history shows that the company was able to sustain its dividend for a decent period of time, its most recent earnings report shows that the company did not make enough earnings to cover its dividend payout. This is an alarming sign for the sustainability of its dividends, as it may mean that Bank of Queenslandis pulling cash from elsewhere to keep its shareholders happy.

Over the next 3 years, EPS is forecast to expand by 193.9%. Despite the current payout ratio being slightly elevated, analysts estimate the future payout ratio will be 71% over the same time period, which would make us comfortable with the sustainability of the dividend.

Check out our latest analysis for Bank of Queensland

Dividend Volatility

The company's dividend history has been marked by instability, with at least one cut in the last 10 years. Since 2015, the dividend has gone from A$0.72 total annually to A$0.40. This works out to be a decline of approximately 5.7% per year over that time. Declining dividends isn't generally what we look for as they can indicate that the company is running into some challenges.

The Dividend's Growth Prospects Are Limited

Given that the track record hasn't been stellar, we really want to see earnings per share growing over time. Over the past five years, it looks as though Bank of Queensland's EPS has declined at around 4.4% a year. If earnings continue declining, the company may have to make the difficult choice of reducing the dividend or even stopping it completely - the opposite of dividend growth. Earnings are predicted to grow over the next year, but we would remain cautious until a track record of earnings growth is established.

Bank of Queensland's Dividend Doesn't Look Sustainable

Overall, this is probably not a great income stock, even though the dividend is being raised at the moment. The payments are bit high to be considered sustainable, and the track record isn't the best. This company is not in the top tier of income providing stocks.

It's important to note that companies having a consistent dividend policy will generate greater investor confidence than those having an erratic one. At the same time, there are other factors our readers should be conscious of before pouring capital into a stock. For example, we've picked out 3 warning signs for Bank of Queensland that investors should know about before committing capital to this stock. Is Bank of Queensland not quite the opportunity you were looking for? Why not check out our selection of top dividend stocks.

data.title: Is Weakness In PolyNovo Limited (ASX:PNV) Stock A Sign That The Market Could be Wrong Given Its Strong Financial Prospects?
data.description:
PolyNovo (ASX:PNV) has had a rough month with its share price down 20%. However, stock prices are usually driven by a company’s financial performance over the long term, which in this case looks quite promising. In this article, we decided to focus on PolyNovo's ROE.

Return on equity or ROE is a key measure used to assess how efficiently a company's management is utilizing the company's capital. Put another way, it reveals the company's success at turning shareholder investments into profits.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

How Is ROE Calculated?

Return on equity can be calculated by using the formula:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for PolyNovo is:

16% = AU$13m ÷ AU$83m (Based on the trailing twelve months to June 2025).

The 'return' refers to a company's earnings over the last year. One way to conceptualize this is that for each A$1 of shareholders' capital it has, the company made A$0.16 in profit.

View our latest analysis for PolyNovo

Why Is ROE Important For Earnings Growth?

So far, we've learned that ROE is a measure of a company's profitability. Based on how much of its profits the company chooses to reinvest or "retain", we are then able to evaluate a company's future ability to generate profits. Assuming everything else remains unchanged, the higher the ROE and profit retention, the higher the growth rate of a company compared to companies that don't necessarily bear these characteristics.

PolyNovo's Earnings Growth And 16% ROE

To start with, PolyNovo's ROE looks acceptable. Especially when compared to the industry average of 12% the company's ROE looks pretty impressive. Probably as a result of this, PolyNovo was able to see an impressive net income growth of 62% over the last five years. We believe that there might also be other aspects that are positively influencing the company's earnings growth. For instance, the company has a low payout ratio or is being managed efficiently.

As a next step, we compared PolyNovo's net income growth with the industry, and pleasingly, we found that the growth seen by the company is higher than the average industry growth of 20%.

Earnings growth is an important metric to consider when valuing a stock. It’s important for an investor to know whether the market has priced in the company's expected earnings growth (or decline). This then helps them determine if the stock is placed for a bright or bleak future. Is PolyNovo fairly valued compared to other companies? These 3 valuation measures might help you decide.

Is PolyNovo Making Efficient Use Of Its Profits?

PolyNovo doesn't pay any regular dividends to its shareholders, meaning that the company has been reinvesting all of its profits into the business. This is likely what's driving the high earnings growth number discussed above.

Conclusion

In total, we are pretty happy with PolyNovo's performance. In particular, it's great to see that the company is investing heavily into its business and along with a high rate of return, that has resulted in a sizeable growth in its earnings. With that said, the latest industry analyst forecasts reveal that the company's earnings growth is expected to slow down. To know more about the company's future earnings growth forecasts take a look at this free report on analyst forecasts for the company to find out more.
